coredata.dc:title: Microbial and quality improvement of boiled gansi dish using carbon dots combined with radio frequency treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Linlin
Zhang, Min
Bhandari, Bhesh
Bai, Baosong
coredata.dc:description: Use of carbon dots (CDs) combined with radio frequency (RF) was applied to pasteurize and reduce the microorganism population in order to improve the quality of boiled gansi dish. CDs were prepared from banana using hydrothermal method, and characterized by using TEM, XRD and FTIR. The minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) test showed CDs can efficiently inactivate Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis). This study also evaluated the effectiveness of five treatments, including CDs alone, CDs combined RF (CDRF) heating for different time (8 min, 12 min, and 16 min), and high pressure steam (HPS) sterilization of boiled gansi dish inoculated with B. subtilis. After CDRF treated for 8 min, 12 min, and 16 min, the center temperature of samples reached to 78.92, 87.77 and 93.82 °C, and the colony forming units (CFU) of B. subtilis reduced by 2.13, 3.62, and 4.63 log, respectively. Samples with CDRF12 treatment, exhibited better product quality as evidenced by reduced loss of texture, flavor, and sensory as compared with HPS sample. The results indicated that CDRF treatment has a great potential to produce packaged boiled gansi dish with high product quality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
RF pasteurization
Boiled gansi dish
Microbiological amount
Texture

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and inoculation of boiled gansi dish

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China Stem Cell and Translational Research

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of banana CDs. (a) TEM micrograph, (b) particle size distribution, (c) FTIR spectrum, (d) XRD pattern spectrum, and (e) fluorescence spectrum.
The center and surface temperature curves of samples during RF pasteurization.
The colony forming units (CFUs) of B. subtilis in samples with different treatments.
Effects of various treatments on the hardness and chewiness of gansi samples.
(a) Water distribution in gansi samples, (b) the protein content in soup.
(a) Response values of different boiled gansi dish samples, and (b) PCA for different boiled gansi dish samples by electronic nose.
The standard of sensory evaluation of boiled gansi dish.

row.entry: Gansi has complete shape, uniform length and no breakage (20–30 points)
Pure in fragrances of bean and chicken, long in aftertaste time (20–30 points)
Dense in texture, moderate in hardness and good in elasticit (30–40 points)
A small portion gansi breaks into small pieces (10–20 points)
Tasteless in fragrances of bean and chicken, without aftertaste time (10–20 points)
Low in hardness, normal in chewiness and elasticity (15–30 points)
Most gansi breaks into small pieces (0–10 points)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sensors and sensing types of the e-Nose.
Minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of banana CDs against different bacterial strains.
Transverse relaxation times (T2) and water proportions (A2) of five types of gansi samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Means ± SD with the different letters within a column represents significant difference (P < 0.05). The same as below.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sensory quality changes of boiled gansi dish after different treatments.

head.ce:title: Microbial and quality improvement of boiled gansi dish using carbon dots combined with radio frequency treatment

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Use of carbon dots (CDs) combined with radio frequency (RF) was applied to pasteurize and reduce the microorganism population in order to improve the quality of boiled gansi dish. CDs were prepared from banana using hydrothermal method, and characterized by using TEM, XRD and FTIR. The minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) test showed CDs can efficiently inactivate Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis). This study also evaluated the effectiveness of five treatments, including CDs alone, CDs combined RF (CDRF) heating for different time (8 min, 12 min, and 16 min), and high pressure steam (HPS) sterilization of boiled gansi dish inoculated with B. subtilis. After CDRF treated for 8 min, 12 min, and 16 min, the center temperature of samples reached to 78.92, 87.77 and 93.82 °C, and the colony forming units (CFU) of B. subtilis reduced by 2.13, 3.62, and 4.63 log, respectively. Samples with CDRF12 treatment, exhibited better product quality as evidenced by reduced loss of texture, flavor, and sensory as compared with HPS sample. The results indicated that CDRF treatment has a great potential to produce packaged boiled gansi dish with high product quality.

ce:section.ce:para: Boiled gansi dish is a Chinese traditional dish deeply loved by people because of its “fine knife technique and fresh taste”. It is prepared by slicing and cutting the dried tofu into julienne strips, and then cooked with soup (Zhang, 2012). The boiled gansi dish contains similar nutrients as soy milk and tofu but has a short shelf life. The packaged boiled gansi dishes are prone to spoilage and deterioration. Hence, in order to maximise the shelf-life and preserve the quality of product during the storage period, the choice of sterilization method is very critical. At present, the widely used sterilization method is high pressure steam (HPS, 121 °C, 30 min), but this sterilization method has negatively impact on quality (nutrition, texture, color and flavor) of the dishes (Dasan et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2019). Therefore, it is essential to explore novel pasteurization technology, that uses low-thermal or non-thermal methods, and ensures the microbiological safety while maintaining the original quality of food maximally.

Carbon dots (CDs) have lately gained tremendous attention in many fields as new nanomaterials owing to their excellent biocompatibility, non-toxicity (Mehta et al., 2014; Sahu et al., 2012), high photo-stability, and good water solubility (Ji et al., 2018; Lim et al., 2015). CDs are a kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a particle size of less than 10 nm (Yao et al., 2019), and have unique morphology, surface functional groups and physico-chemical properties (De and Karak, 2013; Fang et al., 2016). At present, hydrothermal method is the mostly used method for CDs synthesis and research. This is because of the fact that the hydrothermal synthesis method can utilize the raw materials from a wide range of sources, such as vitamin C, oranges, onions, egg shells and other organic biological materials and is a low cost method (Fan et al., 2020).

CDs have been studied in food science areas for detecting food-borne pathogenic bacteria such as Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes), E. coli, and Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium), compounds in food such as melanine, vainillin, pesticides, and hydrogen peroxide (Bonilla et al., 2016). Additionally, its antibacterial activity has also been investigated. For examples, Du et al. (2020) studied antibacterial effect of carbon nanodots and revealed its antimicrobial effects against S. aureus, E. coli and B. subtilis. Singh et al. (2017) demonstrated curcumin quantum dots synthetized with curcumin exhibited antibacterial activities against Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), Klebsiella pneumoniae (K. pneumoniae), Enterococcus faecalis (E. faecalis), S. aureus, and E. coli. Fan et al. (2019) reported that CDs have the ability to inhibit E. coli and S. aureus. They also used the mixture of CDs and chitosan for coating of fresh-cut cucumber, and demonstrated that this coating can constrain the growth of mold and yeast, and preserve the quality during storage. The mixture of CDs and chitosan was also found effective as an antibacterial agent and prolonged the shelf life of soybean milk (Zhao et al., 2020).

Radio frequency (RF) is a promising heating technology and has been widely used in food processing field (Peng et al., 2019). It provides a rapid and volumetric heat generation throughout the materials because of friction generated by dipole rotation and ionic conduction and has the advantages of large penetration depth and high energy utilization rate (Dag et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019). Recently, RF has been applied as a new pasteurization technology to inactivate pathogenic microorganisms in foods, such as S. typhimurium in shell eggs (Yang and Geveke, 2020), B. subtilis spores in soy milk (Uemura et al., 2010), S. typhimurium (Zhang et al., 2020) and Bacillus cereus spores (Jiao et al., 2019) in red pepper powder, Enterococcus faecium in wheat flour (Xu et al., 2020), and food-borne pathogenic bacteria in beef (Schlisselberg et al., 2013). Meanwhile, RF has been found to be an efficient pasteurization technology for low-moisture materials to minimize safety risk without affecting sensory quality (Xu et al., 2020). Previously, several studies of RF pasteurization for packaged foods have also been reported such as stewed pork with carrots (Xu et al., 2019) and Twice-cooked pork dish (Xu et al., 2018). All above investigations illustrated that RF pasteurization is an emerging thermal technology that possesses great potential for sterilization of packaged foods.

In this experiment, CDs was used in combination with RF heating, and the sterilization effect was compared with that of HPS. The effect of this mild pasteurization technology on the physico-chemical indicators, microbial safety and product quality of packaged boiled gansi dish was studied. The results can provide the theoretical and practical understandings for industrial manufacturing of boiled gansi dish.

Dried tofu was purchased from Yangzhou Weiyang Bean Food Co., Ltd. Rapeseed oil, concentrated Tang Bao, salt and chicken essence were donated by Yechun Food Co., Ltd. Banana was bought from Auchan supermarket in Wuxi, China.

The CDs were synthesized following the method of Mehta et al. (2014) with some modifications. A paste was made with 30 g of bananas and 80 mL of deionized water. 20 mL of ethanol and 20 mL of paste were taken into a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor and heated at 150 °C for 5 h. The product was centrifuged (3100 ×g, 20 min), the supernatant was preserved and filtered with a filter (0.22 μm membrane), dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da), then freeze-dried for further characterization. Finally, the CDs powder were dispersed in deionized water at a concentration of 2 mg/mL and placed in a 4 °C refrigerator for use.

Morphology of the CDs was measured using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). Particle size of the CDs was measured using a particle size analyzer (Nano ZS-90, Malvern, UK). X-ray pattern spectrum was determined by a X-ray diffractometer (XRD, EDXS, Oxford Instruments, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained by a FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet iS10, Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., USA). Fluorescent spectrum was obtained by a F-2700 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan).

B. subtilis, S. aureus and E. coli were inoculated in sterile Luria-Bertani (LB) broth and incubated in an oscillating incubator (at 37 ± 1 °C) for 12 h. Then, bacterial strains were rinsed twice with normal saline and put in 96 well plate at a concentration of 106 cells/mL. Each well contained 100 μL of LB broth and 50 μL of bacteria cell suspension. The concentrations of CDs in each well were 0, 0.03, 0.06, 0.12, 0.24, 0.48, 0.96 and 1.92 mg/mL. The analysis was performed in triplicate. All bacterial cells were cultivated at 37 ± 1 °C for 24 h. Optical density (OD) of the sample was determined by a high performance microplate spectrophotometer (BioTek Instruments, Winooski, USA) at 595 nm wavelength. The inactivation effect by different treatments can be evaluated following Travlou et al. (2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and inoculation of boiled gansi dish
ce:section.ce:para: Pre-made soup preparation: Water was put in the pot and added with rapeseed oil, concentrated Tangbao and salt, boiled, added with chicken essence, homogenized for 1 min and finally packaged for use.

Boiled gansi dish preparation: Dried tofu was cut into julienne (called gansi), blanched in boiling water, drained, put in retort pouch, added with pre-made soup, and finally cooled. Each bag of samples contained 60 g gansi and 60 g soup. After cooling, 100 μL of the B. subtilis suspension prepared above was inoculated into the boiled gansi dish, and the initial count of B. subtilis in sample was approximately 6.5 log CFU/mL. Subsequently, except control sample and un-inoculated sample, all samples were added with CDs, and the final concentration of CDs was 0.24 mg/mL. The packaged samples were kept in a 4 °C refrigerator for 3 h before various treatments were carried out.

In this experiment, six groups of boiled gansi dish samples were designed. The first group was the control group without any treatment. The second group was the CDs group only with CDs added. Groups 3, 4 and 5 were CDs + RF treatment groups, which used a RF heating apparatus (27 MHz, 6 kW, Model SO6B, Strayfield Co. Ltd., Berkshire, UK) to heat samples, named CDRF8 group, CDRF12 group, and CDRF16 group. The plate spacing was set at 120 mm, and the samples were heated for 8 min, 12 min and 16 min, respectively. All samples were placed in the same position of RF heating container for treatment. The center temperatures of samples during the RF treatment were recorded at 2 min intervals using a fiber optic sensor. The surface temperatures of samples were recorded using an infrared thermal imaging camera (Fluke Ti10 Multi-Purpose Imager, Fluke, USA) and monitored within 5 s. The sixth group was the HPS group, which was sterilized using an autoclave at 121 °C for 30 min.

The amount of B. subtilis in boiled gansi dish samples after different treatment was determined following Moreira et al. (2011). Approximately 10 g of gansi and 15 g of soup were diluted with 225 mL of sterilized saline solution and homogenized. The 10-fold dilutions were continuously diluted at a ratio of 1:10. One milliliter of dilution with different dilution ratios were mixed with plate count agar in the plate, and the typical colonies were recorded after 48 h of cultivation at 37 °C. Un-inoculated sample was also counted using the same method to remove interference from any possible background microorganisms. All microbial enumeration processes were repeated three times and duplicate plates were used for each dilution gradient.

Texture parameters of the sample, such as chewiness and hardness, were determined by a texture analyzer (TA-XT plus, Stable Micro systems, London, UK) following the TPA method. The following test parameters were used: probe, P/36; pre-test speed, 2.0 mm/s; test speed, 0.5 mm/s; post-test speed, 0.5 mm/s; compression ratio, 45%; trigger force, 5.0 g; time, 3 s; twice compression. Each sample test was repeated 6 times. The dried tofu cut into julienne was not conducive to the determination of texture, so for this part of the experiment, the dried tofu was cut into slices instead of julienne.

The water distribution in gansi samples were determined using a low field nuclear magnetic resonance (LF-NMR) analyzer (PQ001, Niumag, Shanghai, China) as suggested by Wang et al. (2018). Each sample was set in a special glass container for testing at 32 °C. The transverse relaxation time (T2) of samples was measured using a Carre–Purcelle–Meiboome–Gill pulse sequence. The main parameters were used as follows: 90° pulse time, 6.52 μs; 180° pulse time, 12.00 μs; time waiting, 3000 ms; number of echoes, 3000; sampling point, 240,018; time echo, 0.8 ms; spectral width, 100 kHz; number of scans, 4.
The protein content in boiled gansi soup was measured by the Kjeldahl method (AOAC International, 2012; Method 955.04).

A sensory evaluation was implemented in the Sensory Laboratory. The sensory evaluation team was made up of 20 trained panelists. The scoring standard rating was used as suggested by Xu et al. (2018) with some modification (Table 1 ). All samples were evaluated for their taste, flavor and appearance.

The flavor changes of different boiled gansi samples tested with an e-Nose (Ruifen Trading Co., China) with following parameters, 1 L/min of airflow, 180 s of cleaning time, and 180 s of testing time. Fourteen sensors and sensing types of the e-Nose are listed in Table 2 .
Experimental data were analyzed by IBM SPSS software 19.0, and shown as mean ± standard deviation (SD). OriginPro 2016 64Bit was applied to do graphing. Each sample measurement was repeated 3 times.

In this experiment, banana was used as substrate to synthesize CDs. The results are shown in Fig. 1 . TEM image showed that the morphology of the banana CDs was monodispersed and spherical in nature (Fig. 1a). Fan et al. (2019) found that the particle size of kelp CDs was 0.54–0.83 nm. Similarly, the diameter of the banana CDs synthesized in this experiment was mostly less than 1 nm (Fig. 1b). The FTIR spectrum of banana CDs as shown in Fig. 1c, indicated the presence of the various functional groups. The spectrum exhibited a peak at 3394 cm−1 corresponding to OH stretching vibration, the peak at 2928 cm−1 corresponding to CH stretching vibration, the peaks at 1631 cm−1 and 1701 cm−1 corresponding to CC, CO stretching bending vibrations, the peaks at 1028 cm−1 belonging to CO stretching vibration. As illustrated by a XRD pattern (Fig. 1d), banana CDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak at 2 θ = 20.8° which is attributed to the lattice spacing of the graphitic carbon, indicating that the CDs contain graphite-like structures. As it can be seen in Fig. 1(e), the maximum emission wavelength is located at 460 nm as the excitation wavelength is 370 nm which is attributed to size differences and multiple surface emissive trap statuses of CDs.

Double dilution method was applied to confirm the antibacterial property of banana CDs. The results (Table 3 ) showed that banana CDs effectively inactivated Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and B. subtilis) and Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli). The MIC of B. subtilis, S. aureus and E. coli were 0.24, 0.12, and 0.12 mg/mL, respectively, indicating that the CDs displayed better antibacterial effect on E. coli and S. aureus than on B. subtilis. Du et al. (2020) has reported similar results stating that the MICs of carbon nanodots (CNDs) against B. subtilis, S. aureus and E. coli were 0.768, 0.384, and 0.192 mg/mL, respectively. For other antibiotics such as spectinomycin and neomycin were reported exhibiting MICs of >0.128 mg/mL and 0.512 mg/mL against E. coli (Travlou et al., 2018). The reported MICs of some nanometer antibacterial agents such as chitosan nanoparticles, eugenolgrafted chitosan nanoparticles and carvacrol-grafted chitosan nanoparticles against E. coli were 0.5–1, 0.25–0.5, and 0.5–1 mg/mL, against S. aureus were 0–1, 0.5, and 0.5–1 mg/mL (Du et al., 2020). The reported MIC of ampicillin against S. aureus was 2.5 mg/mL (Xu et al., 2014) and MICs of GO-Ag, CNT-Ag and Ag colloid against B. subtilis were 4, 0.5, and 1.69 mg/mL (Yun et al., 2013). Obviously, from this comparison, it can be seen that the antibacterial activity of CDs against tested bacterial cells (S. aureus, B. subtilis, and E. coli) was better than some common nanoparticles, and CDs showed better inhibitory effect than some antibiotics.

The center and surface temperature curves during RF heating are shown in Fig. 2 . In the initial phase of RF treatment, the center temperature of the sample was much higher than surface temperature (P < 0.05), and by the end of the heating period, this difference gradually decreased. The final center and surface temperature of the CDRF sample treated for 16 min was 93.82 °C and 92.01 °C, while that treated for 12 min was 91.12 °C and 89.03 °C. There was no significant difference between center and surface temperature of these two treatment times. In the RF heating process, the thermal resistance retort pouch with stronger insulating property can be helpful to maintain the uniformity of the internal temperature in samples (Jiao et al., 2015). The more uniform the temperature distribution in the sample, the better the pasteurization effect is.

Some studies reported that B. subtilis derived from soil may survive in soy milk (Adebayo-Tayo et al., 2009) and can cause deterioration of soybean products produced from soybean milk, such as tofu (Uemura et al., 2010). As the main material of boiled gansi dish is dried tofu, this bacterium was selected for testing the inactivation effect of CDRF pasteurization. The inactivation of B. subtilis in boiled gansi dishes by different treatments is shown in Fig. 3 . Among all treatments, the effect of CDs treatment was the weakest, only reduced 1.60 log of B. subtilis. HPS treatment was the most effective sterilization method as no colony was detected. CDs + RF treatment reduced the CFU of B. subtilis by 2.13, 3.62, and 4.63 log after heating for 8 min, 12 min, and 16 min, respectively, which manifested that the pasteurization effect of RF is related to the heating time. Xu et al. (2018) have reported similar results that the extension of the RF pasteurization time improves the bactericidal effect for the twice-cooked pork dish samples. The heating uniformity of the boiled gansi dish samples with RF heating for 16 min was better than that for RF treatment for 12 min, so the pasteurization effect was also better.

The texture parameters, such as hardness, chewiness, resilience, cohesiveness and springiness, are the most direct indicators for judging the quality and palatability of dishes. In comparison with the control group, the hardness and chewiness of the samples (Fig. 4 ) in the HPS group were obviously decreased (P < 0.05), which indicated that the traditional HPS sterilization greatly damaged the texture. The high temperature treatment can cause the bound water inside the sample to vaporize and escape, making the three-dimensional network structure of the protein gel getting destroyed, and thus reducing the hardness and chewiness of the dried tofu (Lin et al., 2013). The hardness and chewiness values of the samples treated with RF for 8 min and 12 min showed no significant difference with those of the control group, however, the hardness of sample treated with RF for 16 min was significantly reduced (P < 0.05). The differences in hardness and chewiness between RF 16 min samples and HPS samples were not significant (P > 0.05). The above results indicated that the influence of RF pasteurization on the texture was related to the heating time. In this experiment, 8 min and 12 min of RF heating can maintain the texture of samples, however, 16 min of RF heating significantly (P < 0.05) changed the texture of samples, resulting in similar results to HPS sterilization.

The water distribution in gansi samples after RF pasteurization and HPS sterilization was evaluated by LF-NMR. As displayed in Fig. 5a the NMR spectrums of all samples exhibited three relaxation peaks, which are named as T21, T22, and T23. Following the suggestion of Li et al. (2014), the first peak (T21) can be regarded as water molecules tightly bound to protein, and could be considered as a part of protein structure. The second peak (T22) can be attributed to water molecules close to the surface of the network structures, which are easily affected by the interaction between the water molecules and the protein network. This kind of water molecules exists in the space of tertiary structure and quaternary structure of protein and in some small meshes in microstructure. The third peak (T23) is represented water molecules that is away from the protein network and less influenced by the protein structure, which exists in the gaps and larger pores in three-dimensional network. As can be seen from Table 4 , T22 water was the dominant population in all samples, of approximately 90%. In comparison with the control group, the A22 proportion of the HPS sample was obviously reduced (P < 0.05), and the A23 proportion was significantly improved (P < 0.05). This may be because after the HPS treatment, the water inside the sample was vaporized, expanded and escaped, thereby forming larger pores in the sample (Lin et al., 2013), which resulted in a loose structure, and promoted the transformation of T22 water into T23 water. RF treated for 8 min, 12 min, and 16 min also reduced the A22 proportion, increased the A23 proportion of samples, but there was a little difference in value.

Fig. 5b shows the influence of five types of bactericidal methods on protein content in soup of boiled gansi dish. Compared with the control group and CDs group, RF pasteurization for 16 min and HPS sterilization significantly increased the protein content of soup (P < 0.05). HPS sterilization improved the protein value of soup from 1.13 to 2.85 mg/mL. but reduced the protein value of gansi. The results further indicated that RF 16 min and HPS caused structural changes in gansi sample and some proteins in gansi were dissolved in water (soup).

As shown in Table 5 , the total sensory scores of the samples of CDs, CDRF8 and CDRF12 groups were slightly lower than control sample. The appearance, flavor and taste score of samples in CDRF16 and HPS groups were obviously reduced (P < 0.05). The total score of samples in CDRF16 group was 72.98, while in HPS it was only 66.24. A qualified boiled gansi dish should have a complete shape, uniform length, pure fragrances of bean and chicken, dense texture, moderate hardness and good elasticity. If the gansi breaks into small pieces, or textural structure becomes loose, it will reduce the consumer appeal. In this study, with the increasing time of RF treatment and high temperature of HPS sterilization, the protein structure and flavor substance of gansi were changed, affecting the texture and appearance, even the flavor of the product. Among all treatments, the impact of HPS treatment on product quality of boiled gansi dish was the most obvious, followed by RF treated for 16 min.

Fig. 6a shows flavor response of boiled gansi dish as analyzed by e-nose. Fourteen kinds of sensors showed no evident differences among the control sample, CDs sample, CDRF8 sample and CDRF12 sample. In comparison with the control sample, CDRF16 treatment and HPS treatment resulted in the higher signals of S1, S2, S5, S6, S8, S9, S10 and S13 sensors, which indicated that RF16 treatment and HPS treatment changed the flavor of boiled gansi dish samples. The flavor differences of various groups were discriminated by means of principal component analysis (PCA). As displayed in Fig. 6b, the cumulative contribution of principal component 1 and principal component 2 reached to 96.4%, indicating that the overall information of samples can be represented (Zhao et al., 2017). The graphs of RF 16 min sample and HPS sample were far apart from the control sample, which indicate that the flavor in the last two groups differ from that in control group. The results of the electronic nose were highly consistent with the sensory evaluation results, inferring that quality of the boiled gansi dish samples treated with CDRF16 and HPS have changed obviously.

This study demonstrated that banana can be used as a substrate to synthesize CDs, and the banana CDs can efficiently inactivate B. subtilis, S. aureus and E. coli as shown by the MIC. The CDRF treatment effectively reduced the CFU of B. subtilis inoculated in boiled gansi samples and the effectiveness was a function of duration of RF treatment. CDRF12 and CDRF16 samples had better RF heating uniformity, so consequently showing the better pasteurization effect. Therefore, use of CDs combined with RF pasteurization for an optimum time has potential to be a substitute method to traditional HPS sterilization for improving the microbial, physical and sensorial qualities of boiled gansi dish.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial supports from the China Key Research Programs (Contract No. 2017YFD0400501), the 111 Project (BP0719028), Yangzhou City Agricultural Key R&D Program (No. YZ2019034), Jiangsu Province (China) “Collaborative Innovation Center for Food Safety and Quality Control” Industry Development Program, Jiangsu Province Key Laboratory Project of Advanced Food Manufacturing Equipment and Technology (No. FMZ201803), Research Innovation Project for Postgraduates in Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX20_1846), all of which enabled us to carry out this study.
